walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Score Board
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get Nations set up. First, find the large Score Board and place it in the middle of the table. This board has a lot going on. You will see the Round Track across the top showing four ages: Antiquity, Medieval, Renaissance, and Industrial, with two rounds per age for eight rounds total. Below that is the Heritage Track where you will track your accumulated Books. There is also a Military Strength Track, a Stability Track, a Player Order Track, spaces for Architects, a War slot, and an Event Card slot. It looks like a lot, but each track has a clear purpose and we will cover them as we go.

walkthrough.title: Place the Progress Board
walkthrough.text: Now find the Progress Board and set it next to the Score Board. This is the marketplace where you will buy cards during the game. It has three rows and multiple columns. The top row costs 3 Gold per card, the middle row costs 2 Gold, and the bottom row costs 1 Gold. The number of columns you use depends on your player count. For 2 players use columns 1-4, for 3 players use columns 1-5, for 4 players use columns 1-6, and for 5 players use all 7 columns. Leave the board empty for now. You will fill it with Progress Cards at the start of each round.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Progress Card Decks
walkthrough.text: Find all the Progress Cards. These are the big decks with historical buildings, military units, wonders, advisors, and more on them. Sort them by age. Each card has a Roman numeral on it: I for Antiquity, II for Medieval, III for Renaissance, and IV for Industrial. Shuffle each age's deck separately and place them in four face-down piles. For your first game, use only the base cards. Leave out cards marked with the advanced symbol or expert symbol. Set the Age II, III, and IV decks aside. You will only need Age I to start.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Event Card Decks
walkthrough.text: Now find the Event Cards. These are a separate, smaller set of cards, also sorted by age. Shuffle each age's Event deck separately and stack them face down. Place the Age I Event deck next to the Score Board. Set the Age II, III, and IV Event decks aside. Event Cards drive the narrative of each round. They tell you how much famine hits, what historical events happen, and how many extra Architects are available.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Resource Supply
walkthrough.text: Gather all the resource tokens and place them in a central supply within everyone's reach. There are four types of resources: Gold tokens, Food tokens, Stone tokens, and Book tokens. Each comes in denominations of 1, 3, and 10. There are also 50-Book tokens for when someone's heritage gets really high. You will also need the VP tokens in denominations of 1, 3, and 10. Place all of these in organized piles so everyone can easily make change during the game.

walkthrough.title: Place the Architects
walkthrough.text: Find the 15 Architect pieces. These are the small tokens you will use to build Wonders. Place them all next to the Score Board. This is the Architect Supply. During setup, place some Architects on the Architect spaces of the Score Board based on player count: 0 for solo, 1 for 2 players, 2 for 3-4 players, or 3 for 5 players. More will be added each round from Event Cards.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Boards
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a Player Board. For your first game, everyone should use the A-side. Each board shows a different historical nation: Egypt, China, Greece, Rome, or Persia. The board has slots for your Advisor, two Colony spaces, five Building and Military spaces, a Wonder under construction space, five completed Wonder spaces, and a Population Track. The Population Track is divided into a Food section and a Stability section, each with four spaces for Workers. Your nation's name and starting resources are printed on the board.

walkthrough.title: Collect Starting Resources and Workers
walkthrough.text: Look at the bottom of your Player Board. It shows your nation's starting resources: Gold, Food, Stone, and Books. Take those resources from the supply and place them in your Resource Area on your board. Each nation also starts with a pre-printed Advisor, some Colony and Building cards, and a number of Workers on your Population Track. Your starting Workers are already shown on the board. Take the appropriate number of Worker pieces in your color and place them as indicated. Your leftover Workers sit on the Population Track.

walkthrough.title: Determine Player Order
walkthrough.text: Randomly determine the starting player order. You can roll dice, draw numbers, or use any method you like. The first player places one of their colored discs on the number 1 space of the Heritage Track on the Score Board, the second player on number 2, and so on. The last player's disc ends up on top, which matters for tiebreaking on the Heritage Track. Each player also places a disc on the corresponding Player Order Track number.

walkthrough.title: Place the Round Marker
walkthrough.text: Take the white round marker and place it on the A space of Age I on the Round Track. This is your first round. The track shows two rounds per age: A and B. After round B of each age, you will score Books. The game ends after round 8, which is round B of the Industrial age.

walkthrough.title: Choose Difficulty Levels
walkthrough.text: Each player independently chooses a difficulty level by placing a disc on the Score Board. The four levels are Chieftain, Prince, King, and Emperor. This is a built-in handicap system. Newer players should pick Chieftain, which gives the most bonus resources during the Growth step. Experienced players can challenge themselves with Emperor, which gives the least. This means players of different skill levels can play together and have a balanced experience. For your first game, everyone should pick Chieftain.

walkthrough.title: Set Military and Stability Tracks
walkthrough.text: Each player places one disc on value 0 of the Military Strength Track and one disc on value 0 of the Stability Track. These tracks will fluctuate throughout the game as you buy Military cards, deploy Workers, and deal with Events. Military Strength caps at 40 and Stability caps at 15. Stability can go negative, which puts you in Revolt. Place the black War Marker in the War slot on the Score Board. You are now ready to play.

summary.title: Lay Out the Score Board
bullets: Place Score Board centrally — includes Round Track, Heritage Track, Military/Stability Tracks
Also has Player Order Track, Architect spaces, War slot, Event Card slot

summary.title: Place the Progress Board
bullets: Place next to Score Board — 3 rows: top costs 3 Gold, middle 2 Gold, bottom 1 Gold
Column count by players: 2p=4 cols, 3p=5, 4p=6, 5p=7

summary.title: Sort the Progress Card Decks
bullets: Sort by age (I-IV), shuffle each separately
First game: use only base cards (no advanced/expert symbols)
Place Age I deck next to Progress Board; set II-IV aside

summary.title: Sort the Event Card Decks
bullets: Sort Event Cards by age (I-IV), shuffle each separately
Place Age I Event deck next to Score Board; set II-IV aside

summary.title: Set Up the Resource Supply
bullets: Central supply: Gold, Food, Stone, Books (denominations 1, 3, 10)
Also place VP tokens (1, 3, 10) and 50-Book tokens within reach

summary.title: Place the Architects
bullets: 15 Architect tokens go next to Score Board (Architect Supply)
Place on Architect spaces: 0 (solo), 1 (2p), 2 (3-4p), 3 (5p)

summary.title: Hand Out Player Boards
bullets: Each player takes a Player Board (A-side for first game)
5 nations: Egypt, China, Greece, Rome, Persia
Board has slots for Advisor, 2 Colonies, 5 Buildings/Military, Wonders, Population Track

summary.title: Collect Starting Resources and Workers
bullets: Take starting Gold, Food, Stone, Books as printed on your nation board
Place Workers on Population Track as shown
Pre-printed Advisor, Colony, Building cards are already on the board

summary.title: Determine Player Order
bullets: Randomly select player order
Place discs on Heritage Track (1st player lowest, last player on top)
Place discs on Player Order Track to match

summary.title: Place the Round Marker
bullets: White round marker on space A of Age I (Antiquity)
8 rounds total: 2 per age (A and B), Books scored after each B round

summary.title: Choose Difficulty Levels
bullets: Each player picks: Chieftain (easiest), Prince, King, Emperor (hardest)
Affects bonus resources during Growth step each round
Built-in handicap — mixed skill groups can play together

summary.title: Set Military and Stability Tracks
bullets: Each player: one disc on Military Strength 0, one on Stability 0
Military caps at 40, Stability caps at 15 (can go negative = Revolt)
Place black War Marker in War slot — ready to play

walkthrough.title: The Flow of a Round
walkthrough.text: Nations is played over 8 rounds, grouped into 4 ages of 2 rounds each. Every round follows the same three-phase structure: Maintenance Phase, Action Phase, and Resolution Phase. The Maintenance Phase is the setup for the round where you refill the Progress Board, grow your population or take bonus resources, reveal the Event Card, and refill Architects. The Action Phase is the core of the game where players take turns buying cards, deploying Workers, and building Wonders. The Resolution Phase handles production, player order adjustment, War, Events, and Famine. At the end of every second round, you also score Books. Let's walk through each phase.

walkthrough.title: Maintenance Phase: Round Marker and Progress Cards
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, move the round marker to the next space on the Round Track. Skip this in the very first round since it is already on the A space. Then refill the Progress Board. Remove any remaining cards from rows 1 and 2. Move any cards left on row 3 down to row 1, pushed to the left so everyone knows they are old cards. Then draw new Progress Cards from the current age's deck and fill all empty spaces on the Progress Board. These cards are your shopping options for the round.

walkthrough.title: Maintenance Phase: Growth
walkthrough.text: Every nation, in reverse player order, makes a choice: grow your population or take bonus resources. To grow, take a Worker from your Population Track and add it to your Resource Area. Each Worker taken either reduces your Stability by 3 immediately or costs 3 Food during Production in the Resolution Phase, depending on which section of the Population Track you took the Worker from. If you choose bonus resources instead, you take Food, Stone, or Gold equal to your difficulty level. Chieftain gets the most and Emperor gets the least. You must pick only one resource type. This is where the difficulty handicap matters.

walkthrough.title: Maintenance Phase: Events and Architects
walkthrough.text: Draw one Event Card from the current age and place it face up on the Event slot of the Score Board. This card shows two Events that will resolve at the end of the round, plus the famine cost in Food and the number of extra Architects available this round. Everyone can see it, so you have the entire Action Phase to prepare for what is coming. Then refill the Architect spaces. Move any unused Architects back to the supply. Place new Architects based on player count plus whatever the Event Card shows. These Architects will be available for everyone to hire for Wonder construction.

walkthrough.title: Action Phase: Overview
walkthrough.text: This is the heart of the game. Players take turns in player order, each taking one action per turn. There are three basic actions: Buy a Progress Card, Deploy a Worker, or Hire an Architect. Some Progress Cards also grant special actions. Once you pass, you are done for the round. You cannot take any more actions after passing. Play continues until everyone has passed. The earlier you pass, the fewer actions you take, but sometimes passing early is strategic to avoid over-committing resources.

walkthrough.title: Action A: Buy a Progress Card
walkthrough.text: To buy a Progress Card, pick one from the Progress Board and pay its Gold cost based on which row it is in: 3 Gold for the top row, 2 Gold for the middle, and 1 Gold for the bottom. The card takes effect immediately. Depending on the card type, you either place it on your Player Board in the matching slot, resolve it and remove it, or place it on the War slot. Your Player Board has limited space for each card type. If you have no room, you must remove an existing card to make space. The removed card is gone for good. Remember to immediately update your Military Strength and Stability when cards are added or removed.

walkthrough.title: Progress Card Types: Buildings and Military
walkthrough.text: Buildings and Military share the same five slots on your Player Board. Buildings produce resources when you have Workers deployed on them during Production. Each deployed Worker produces what is shown at the top of the card. Some Buildings also increase Stability. Military cards increase your Military Strength by a set amount per deployed Worker. They also have a Raid value used for Battles. Military cards often consume resources or reduce Stability as upkeep. Buildings and Military without deployed Workers have no effect at all. When you replace a card with a new one, any Workers on the old card are undeployed back to your Resource Area.

walkthrough.title: Progress Card Types: Colonies, Wars, Battles
walkthrough.text: Colonies have a Military Strength requirement. You must meet or exceed it to buy one. Once bought, the requirement no longer matters. You can hold up to two Colonies. When you get a third, you must remove one of the old ones. Wars are unique. Any player can buy one, but only one War can exist per round. When bought, the War goes on the Score Board War slot, and the War Marker is set to the buyer's current Military Strength. In the Resolution Phase, any nation below that strength suffers defeat. Battles give you an immediate bonus of Books, Food, or Stone equal to your best Military card's Raid value, then the card is removed. You need at least one Worker deployed on a Military to buy a Battle.

walkthrough.title: Progress Card Types: Wonders, Advisors, Golden Ages
walkthrough.text: Wonders go on your Wonder under construction space. You can only build one at a time. If you buy a new Wonder before finishing the old one, the old one and any Architects on it are lost. Wonders are built in sections by hiring Architects and paying Stone. When the last section is done, the Wonder moves to a completed Wonder space and its special effects activate. Advisors go on the Advisor slot. You can only have one at a time unless a card says otherwise. A new Advisor replaces the old one permanently. Golden Ages give you a choice: take the printed resources, or pay a cost in any combination of resources to gain 1 VP. The card is then removed.

walkthrough.title: Action B: Deploy a Worker
walkthrough.text: An empty Building or Military does nothing. To activate it, deploy a Worker onto it. Take a Worker from your Resource Area or from another Building or Military, place it on the card, and pay the deployment cost in Stone. You can only deploy one Worker per turn, but you can undeploy any number of Workers at any time during your turn without using an action. Each Worker multiplies the card's effect. More Workers on a Building means more production. More Workers on a Military means more Military Strength. But deployment costs Stone, and you need to balance your Worker distribution carefully.

walkthrough.title: Action C: Hire an Architect
walkthrough.text: To build a Wonder, you hire Architects from the Score Board one at a time. Take an Architect, place it on the leftmost empty section of your Wonder under construction, and pay that section's Stone cost. Each Wonder has multiple sections. When you fill the last section, the Wonder is complete. Move it to a completed Wonder space on your Player Board and return the Architects to the supply. If no Architects are available, or you have no Wonder under construction, or you cannot afford the Stone cost, you cannot take this action.

walkthrough.title: Resolution Phase: Production
walkthrough.text: After everyone passes, the Resolution Phase begins. First comes Production. All nations produce simultaneously. Go through each resource type in order: Gold, Food, Stone, Books. For each one, add up all the production from your deployed Workers on Buildings, Military, Colonies, Wonders, and Advisors, then subtract any consumption or upkeep. Take or pay the net amount. If you cannot pay a cost, you lose what you have plus 1 VP and 1 Book per missing resource. Nations in Revolt with negative Stability lose 1 Book per negative point and 1 VP.

walkthrough.title: Resolution Phase: Player Order and War
walkthrough.text: After Production, recalculate player order based on Military Strength. Highest goes first. Ties are broken by Stability, then by previous round order. Redistribute the player order cards and update the track. Then resolve War. If a War was bought this round, compare each nation's Military Strength to the War Marker. Nations meeting or exceeding it are safe. Nations below it are defeated: they lose 1 VP and must pay the resource cost shown on the War card. Stability above 0 reduces the resource loss but cannot reduce the VP loss. Then remove the War card.

walkthrough.title: Resolution Phase: Events and Famine
walkthrough.text: Now resolve both Events on the Event Card from top to bottom. Events compare players on some measure like Military Strength or Stability. The player with the most gets a benefit, and the player with the least suffers a penalty. Critical rule: ties for positive effects mean nobody gets the reward. You must win alone. Ties for negative effects mean all tied players suffer the full penalty. All nations with negative Stability are considered tied for lowest Stability. After Events, resolve Famine. Every nation must pay the Food cost shown on the Event Card. If you cannot pay, you lose what you have, plus 1 VP and 1 Book per missing Food.

walkthrough.title: End of Age Scoring: Books
walkthrough.text: At the end of every second round, which is the B round of each age, you score Books. Each nation gains 1 VP for every other nation that has fewer accumulated Books. So if you have the most Books in a 4-player game, you gain 3 VP. If two players tie, neither gets VP for the other. This scoring happens four times over the course of the game, at the end of Antiquity, Medieval, Renaissance, and Industrial. Books are critically important because this scoring compounds over the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after round 8. Do the normal Resolution Phase including the final Books scoring. Then tally final scores across five categories. A: Game VP, the VP tokens you accumulated during the game. B: Colonies, VP printed on your Colony cards. C: Wonders, VP from your completed Wonders including any special bonuses. D: Buildings and Military, VP from Workers deployed on cards as shown by the VP symbols under the card art. The first Worker gets the leftmost value, second gets the next, and so on. Cards without Workers score nothing. E: Resources, add up all your remaining Gold, Food, Stone, and Books plus your Military Strength and Stability. For every full 10 of that total, gain 1 VP. Add all five categories. Highest total wins. Ties are broken by player order.

summary.title: The Flow of a Round
bullets: 8 rounds total, 2 per age (Antiquity, Medieval, Renaissance, Industrial)
Each round: Maintenance Phase, Action Phase, Resolution Phase
Books scored at end of each age (rounds 2, 4, 6, 8)

summary.title: Maintenance Phase: Round Marker and Progress Cards
bullets: Advance round marker (skip in round 1)
Remove cards from rows 1-2, move row 3 to row 1, refill all empty spaces
New cards come from current age's Progress Card deck

summary.title: Maintenance Phase: Growth
bullets: In reverse player order: grow population OR take bonus resources
Grow = take Worker from Population Track (-3 Stability or -3 Food upkeep)
Bonus = take Food, Stone, or Gold equal to difficulty level (Chieftain most, Emperor least)

summary.title: Maintenance Phase: Events and Architects
bullets: Draw 1 Event Card — shows 2 events, famine cost, extra Architects
Events resolve later; visible now so you can prepare
Refill Architect spaces = base amount by player count + Event Card bonus

summary.title: Action Phase: Overview
bullets: Players take turns in player order, one action each
3 actions: Buy Progress Card, Deploy Worker, Hire Architect
Pass = done for the round; cannot resume
Some Progress Cards grant special actions (mark Used)

summary.title: Action A: Buy a Progress Card
bullets: Pay Gold based on row: top=3, middle=2, bottom=1
Card takes effect immediately — place on board, resolve, or set on War slot
Limited slots per type; must remove old card if no room (card lost forever)
Update Military Strength and Stability immediately

summary.title: Progress Card Types: Buildings and Military
bullets: Buildings: Workers produce resources during Production; some give Stability
Military: Workers give Military Strength; cards have Raid value for Battles; upkeep costs
Both share 5 slots; empty cards do nothing; replaced cards undeploy Workers

summary.title: Progress Card Types: Colonies, Wars, Battles
bullets: Colonies: must meet Military Strength requirement to buy; max 2 (3rd replaces one)
Wars: 1 per round max; War Marker set to buyer's strength; weaker nations defeated in Resolution
Battles: gain Books/Food/Stone equal to best Raid value; need Worker on Military; card removed

summary.title: Progress Card Types: Wonders, Advisors, Golden Ages
bullets: Wonders: 1 under construction at a time; built in sections with Architects + Stone; replacing loses old
Advisors: 1 at a time (unless a card says otherwise); new replaces old permanently
Golden Ages: take printed resources OR pay cost for 1 VP; card removed

summary.title: Action B: Deploy a Worker
bullets: Place Worker from Resource Area (or another card) onto Building/Military; pay Stone cost
1 deployment per turn; undeploy any number for free at any time on your turn
Each Worker multiplies card effect (production, strength, etc.)
Workers cannot deploy on Colonies, Wonders, or Advisors

summary.title: Action C: Hire an Architect
bullets: Take Architect from Score Board, place on leftmost empty Wonder section, pay Stone
When last section filled = Wonder complete; move to completed slot, return Architects
Cannot hire if no Architects available, no Wonder under construction, or insufficient Stone

summary.title: Resolution Phase: Production
bullets: All nations produce simultaneously; order: Gold, Food, Stone, Books
Sum production minus consumption for each resource across all cards
Cannot pay = lose what you have + 1 VP + 1 Book per missing resource
In Revolt (negative Stability) = lose 1 Book per negative point + 1 VP

summary.title: Resolution Phase: Player Order and War
bullets: Player order = highest Military Strength first; tiebreak: Stability, then previous order
War: nations below War Marker strength lose 1 VP + resource cost (reduced by Stability, not VP)
VP loss from War cannot be mitigated

summary.title: Resolution Phase: Events and Famine
bullets: Resolve both Events top to bottom; positive rewards require winning alone (no ties)
Negative effects hit all tied-for-last players in full
All nations with negative Stability count as tied for lowest Stability
Famine: all nations pay Food cost on Event Card; shortfall = VP loss + Book loss

summary.title: End of Age Scoring: Books
bullets: After rounds 2, 4, 6, 8: each nation gains 1 VP per opponent with fewer Books
Ties mean neither gets VP for the other
Compounds over 4 scoring rounds — huge long-term value

summary.title: Game End and Final Scoring
bullets: A: Game VP (tokens accumulated during play)
B: Colony VP
C: Wonder VP (including special bonuses)
D: Building/Military VP (from deployed Workers only; card position matters)
E: Resources = (Gold + Food + Stone + Books + Military + Stability) / 10, round down
Highest total wins; ties broken by player order

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with three example players. Player 1 is Egypt, Player 2 is Rome, and Player 3 is Greece. Each player will take different actions so you can see how the key decisions work. We will also see Player 3 make some common beginner mistakes and learn from them. Follow along even if your group has a different number of players. Ready? Let's jump into Antiquity.

walkthrough.title: Maintenance Phase Setup
walkthrough.text: It is Round 1, so the round marker is already on Age I, space A. We skip moving it this round. Now we fill the Progress Board. Draw Progress Cards from the Age I deck and fill every empty space on the board. In a 3-player game, that means columns 1 through 5, across all three rows. Look at what is available. You might see Buildings like Granary or Ziggurat, Military like Hoplites, a Colony like Macedonia, maybe a Wonder like the Pyramids, and an Advisor or two. Everyone take a moment to study the board.

walkthrough.title: Growth Decisions
walkthrough.text: In reverse player order, each nation decides to grow or take bonus resources. Player 3 (Greece) goes first. Greece decides to take bonus resources. Since everyone is on Chieftain difficulty, Greece takes 5 Gold. Smart move. Gold is the currency for buying Progress Cards, and round 1 is all about shopping. Player 2 (Rome) also takes 5 Gold. Player 1 (Egypt) decides to grow population instead, taking a Worker from the Food section of the Population Track. Egypt now has an extra Worker to deploy but will owe 3 extra Food during Production.

walkthrough.title: Events and Architects
walkthrough.text: Draw the first Event Card and place it face up. Let's say it shows a famine cost of 4 Food, one extra Architect, and two Events. Everyone can see what is coming at the end of this round: you will need at least 4 Food or face penalties, and the Events might reward the strongest military or punish the weakest. For Architects, we started with 2 on the board for a 3-player game, and the Event Card adds 1 more, so there are 3 Architects available for Wonder construction this round.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 (Egypt): Buy a Building
walkthrough.text: Player 1 is up first. Egypt scans the Progress Board and spots a Granary on the bottom row for just 1 Gold. The Granary produces 2 Food per deployed Worker. Egypt buys it, pays 1 Gold, and places it on an empty Building slot on the Player Board. Now Egypt needs to deploy a Worker on it to activate it. That will cost Stone, which Egypt will do on a later turn. Buying cheap production Buildings early is a solid foundation move.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 (Rome): Buy Military
walkthrough.text: Player 2 looks at the Event Card. One Event rewards the nation with the highest Military Strength. Rome decides to invest in military. Rome buys Hoplites from the middle row for 2 Gold. The card goes on a Military slot. Hoplites give 3 Military Strength per deployed Worker and have a Raid value of 3. But they also consume 1 Stone per Worker during Production. Military is powerful but expensive to maintain.

walkthrough.title: Player 3 (Greece): The Mistake - Buying Too Expensive
walkthrough.text: Player 3 spots a powerful Wonder on the top row. It costs 3 Gold. Greece buys it, excited about the VP it will provide when completed. But here is the problem: Greece just spent 3 of their 5 Gold on a Wonder that needs multiple Architects and Stone to build. Greece has very little Stone and may not get enough Architects. Meanwhile, the Progress Board has affordable Buildings and Military that would have immediately helped Greece's economy. That Wonder is going to sit under construction for a long time while Egypt and Rome build productive engines.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build Your Economy First
walkthrough.text: The takeaway here: in the early rounds, prioritize buildings that produce resources over flashy Wonders. Wonders are great for VP but they do nothing until they are fully constructed, which can take several rounds and a lot of Stone. A Building with a Worker on it starts producing immediately during the very next Resolution Phase. Get your economy running before you start investing in luxury projects.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 (Egypt): Deploy a Worker
walkthrough.text: Egypt's second turn. Time to activate that Granary. Egypt takes a Worker from the Resource Area, places it on the Granary, and pays 1 Stone for deployment. Now during Production, that Worker will generate 2 Food. Egypt is already thinking ahead to the famine cost of 4 Food shown on the Event Card. Between the Granary production and existing Food, Egypt should be safe. This is smart planning. Always look at the Event Card and make sure you can cover famine before the round ends.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 (Rome): Deploy on Military
walkthrough.text: Rome deploys a Worker on the Hoplites, paying 1 Stone. Rome's Military Strength immediately jumps to 3. Move Rome's disc on the Military Strength Track. Now Rome has Raid value 3 for Battles and is positioned to win any Event that rewards the strongest military. But remember, those Hoplites consume 1 Stone per Worker each round during Production. Rome needs to make sure it has Stone income to sustain this.

walkthrough.title: Player 3 (Greece): The Mistake - Neglecting Food
walkthrough.text: Greece uses their turn to hire an Architect for the Wonder, paying 2 Stone. Fine so far. But Greece has not bought any Food-producing Buildings, has not increased Food production at all, and the Event Card shows 4 Food in famine costs. Greece's starting Food supply is going to get eaten up by famine with nothing coming in to replace it. Player 3 is so focused on the Wonder that they forgot the most basic survival need: feed your people.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Watch the Famine
walkthrough.text: Every single round, you must pay the famine cost. If you cannot pay, you lose the Food you have, plus 1 VP and 1 Book per missing Food. In the early game, losing Books is devastating because Books scoring happens at the end of each age. Before you chase Wonders, Military, or anything else, make sure your Food production can cover famine. A Granary with one Worker solves this problem cheaply. Never forget to feed your people.

walkthrough.title: More Actions: Building the Engine
walkthrough.text: Play continues with each player taking turns. Egypt buys another Building for Stone production and deploys a second Worker. Rome buys a Colony requiring Military Strength of 2, which Rome easily meets with Strength 3. The Colony will provide bonus resources each Production phase. Greece finally buys a Building that produces Food, realizing the mistake. Each action is a chance to strengthen your nation's engine or respond to threats on the Event Card.

walkthrough.title: Player 3 (Greece): The Mistake - Ignoring Military
walkthrough.text: Near the end of the Action Phase, Rome buys a War card. The War Marker is placed at Military Strength 3 to match Rome's current strength. Greece has Military Strength of 0. That means Greece will be defeated by the War during the Resolution Phase: Greece will lose 1 VP and must pay the War's resource cost. Greece could have avoided this by buying even a basic Military card earlier. Player 3 is learning the hard way that you cannot ignore Military entirely.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Maintain Some Military
walkthrough.text: You do not need the highest Military Strength, but you need enough to survive Wars. Any player can buy a War at any time, and if your strength is below the War Marker, you get punished. Even a single Military card with one Worker is enough to avoid the weakest Wars. Think of it as insurance: a small investment that prevents catastrophic losses. Also, Military determines player order for the next round. Going first gives you first pick of the Progress Board.

walkthrough.title: Everyone Passes: Resolution Phase
walkthrough.text: All three players have passed. Now we resolve the round. First, Production: every nation simultaneously calculates its resources. Egypt's Granary Worker produces 2 Food and the Stone Building Worker produces Stone. Rome's Colony produces resources, but the Hoplites consume 1 Stone. Greece's new Food Building produces a little Food. Check that you can pay all your consumption costs. Then adjust player order based on Military Strength. Rome goes to position 1 with Strength 3, Egypt stays at 2, Greece drops to 3.

walkthrough.title: War and Events Resolution
walkthrough.text: Now resolve the War. The War Marker is at Strength 3. Rome meets it exactly, so Rome is safe. Egypt has Military Strength 0 and Greece has 0. Both are defeated. Each loses 1 VP and must pay the War's resource cost. Stability above 0 reduces the resource loss, but the VP loss cannot be avoided. Then resolve the two Events on the Event Card. Maybe the strongest military gains 2 Books and the weakest Stability loses 1 Food. Check who qualifies. Remember, ties for positive effects mean nobody gets the reward.

walkthrough.title: Famine and Books Scoring
walkthrough.text: Finally, famine. Every nation must pay 4 Food as shown on the Event Card. Egypt prepared well and pays easily. Rome has enough from Colony production. Greece is short. Greece pays what they have, loses 1 VP, and loses 1 Book per missing Food. Painful. This is round 1A, so we do not score Books yet. Books scoring happens at the end of round 1B, the second round of Antiquity. But Egypt and Rome are already accumulating Books from production while Greece has been losing them. The gap is widening.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Redemption
walkthrough.text: Round 2 begins. Player 3 has learned three hard lessons: do not chase Wonders before your economy is running, always plan for famine, and maintain some military presence. This round, Greece buys a cheap Building for Food production, deploys a Worker on it immediately, and picks up a basic Military card. Greece even manages to finish the Wonder by the end of the round, but now has the economy to actually sustain the nation going forward. Sometimes the best lessons come from early mistakes.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues for 6 more rounds across Medieval, Renaissance, and Industrial ages. The Progress Cards get more powerful each age, and the costs and stakes increase. Wars hit harder, famine gets more expensive, and Events become more dramatic. But your nation grows too. Buildings produce more resources, Wonders provide powerful abilities, and strategic Advisor selections can change your entire approach. Keep balancing economy, military, and Books, and you will be competitive through the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now seen all three action types, watched the full Maintenance-Action-Resolution cycle, and seen Player 3 learn from three classic beginner mistakes: chasing Wonders too early, ignoring famine, and neglecting military. These are the three most common ways new Nations players fall behind. You understand the rhythm of the game and you are ready to play a full session.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your first full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and the biggest beginner traps to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all card types, resource types, and key terminology.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun building your nation!

summary.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 3 players: Egypt, Rome, Greece
Covers a full first round with all three actions demonstrated
Player 3 (Greece) makes common beginner mistakes

summary.title: Maintenance Phase Setup
bullets: Round 1: marker already placed, skip advancing
Fill Progress Board from Age I deck (5 columns for 3 players)
Study available cards: Buildings, Military, Wonders, Advisors

summary.title: Growth Decisions
bullets: Reverse player order: each nation grows or takes bonus resources
Greece and Rome take 5 Gold (Chieftain level) for buying power
Egypt grows population — extra Worker but more Food upkeep

summary.title: Events and Architects
bullets: Event Card revealed: famine 4 Food, 1 extra Architect, 2 Events
Plan your round around visible Events and famine cost
3 Architects available (2 base + 1 from Event Card)

summary.title: Player 1 (Egypt): Buy a Building
bullets: Egypt buys Granary from bottom row for 1 Gold
Produces 2 Food per Worker — strong early economy card
Cheap production Buildings are the foundation of a good start

summary.title: Player 2 (Rome): Buy Military
bullets: Rome buys Hoplites for 2 Gold — 3 Military Strength per Worker
Raid value 3 enables Battles; but consumes 1 Stone per Worker
Military is powerful but has ongoing upkeep costs

summary.title: Player 3 (Greece): The Mistake - Buying Too Expensive
bullets: Greece spends 3 Gold on a top-row Wonder in round 1
Wonder needs Architects + Stone to complete — useless until done
Meanwhile, affordable Buildings would have built immediate economy

summary.title: Lesson: Build Your Economy First
bullets: Prioritize resource-producing Buildings over Wonders in early rounds
Buildings with Workers produce immediately; Wonders do nothing until complete
Get your economy running before investing in luxury VP projects

summary.title: Player 1 (Egypt): Deploy a Worker
bullets: Egypt deploys Worker on Granary for 1 Stone — now producing 2 Food
Planning ahead: famine costs 4 Food, production helps cover it
Always check Event Card famine and plan Food coverage

summary.title: Player 2 (Rome): Deploy on Military
bullets: Rome deploys on Hoplites for 1 Stone — Military Strength jumps to 3
Immediately update Military Strength Track
Now eligible for Events rewarding strongest military

summary.title: Player 3 (Greece): The Mistake - Neglecting Food
bullets: Greece hires Architect for Wonder instead of building Food production
Famine of 4 Food is coming and Greece has no way to produce more
Focused on Wonder while ignoring basic survival

summary.title: Lesson: Always Watch the Famine
bullets: Famine happens every round — shortfall costs VP + Books
Losing Books early is devastating because of age-end scoring
One cheap Food Building with a Worker solves the problem

summary.title: More Actions: Building the Engine
bullets: Egypt adds Stone production; Rome conquers a Colony
Greece course-corrects with a Food Building
Every action should strengthen your engine or counter threats

summary.title: Player 3 (Greece): The Mistake - Ignoring Military
bullets: Rome buys a War — War Marker set to Strength 3
Greece at Strength 0 will be defeated: loses 1 VP + resources
Could have been avoided with even a basic Military card

summary.title: Lesson: Maintain Some Military
bullets: You need enough Military to survive Wars, not necessarily the most
Any player can buy a War — you must be prepared
Military also determines player order; going first = best card picks

summary.title: Everyone Passes: Resolution Phase
bullets: Production: calculate all resource gains and costs simultaneously
Player order updated by Military Strength (highest goes first)
Check consumption costs — cannot pay = lose VP + Books

summary.title: War and Events Resolution
bullets: War: nations below Marker strength lose 1 VP + resource cost
Stability reduces resource loss but NOT VP loss
Events: positive rewards require winning alone; negatives hit all tied-for-last

summary.title: Famine and Books Scoring
bullets: All nations pay famine Food cost — shortfall = VP + Book loss
Books scoring at end of age (round B): 1 VP per opponent with fewer Books
Economy gaps widen quickly if not addressed early

summary.title: Player 3's Redemption
bullets: Greece buys Food Building, deploys Worker, picks up basic Military
Finishes Wonder by end of round 2 with economy now sustaining it
Best lessons come from early mistakes — course-correct quickly

summary.title: Continue Playing
bullets: 6 more rounds across Medieval, Renaissance, Industrial
Cards get more powerful; costs and stakes increase each age
Balance economy, military, and Books throughout the game

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You have practiced all 3 action types and the full round cycle
Player 3 demonstrated 3 classic mistakes: chasing Wonders, ignoring famine, neglecting military
You understand the rhythm — ready for a full game

summary.title: Before You Begin
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics and beginner traps
Reference the Appendix for card types, resources, and terminology
Ask your personal GM Guide in the Q&A tab for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the mistakes that trip up almost every new Nations player. First, neglecting Food production and getting hammered by famine every round. The VP and Book losses add up fast. Second, ignoring Military entirely, which lets other players buy Wars that devastate you and also puts you last in player order. Third, hoarding Gold without spending it. Gold is only useful for buying cards; leftover Gold at game end is worth very little. Fourth, chasing expensive Wonders before your economy can support them. And fifth, forgetting that Books are scored four times over the game, not just once. If you fall behind on Books early, you lose VP every single age.

walkthrough.title: First Round Priorities
walkthrough.text: Your first round sets the tone for your entire game. Buy at least one Building that produces Food or Stone and deploy a Worker on it immediately. Check the Event Card famine cost and make sure your Food production can cover it. If there is an affordable Military card, consider grabbing it for War protection and player order benefits. Avoid expensive top-row cards in round 1 unless they solve an immediate problem. The bottom row has great deals for 1 Gold. Think of the first round as laying a foundation, not building a monument.

walkthrough.title: Gold Management
walkthrough.text: Gold is your shopping currency. You earn it through Building production and the Growth bonus. In the early game, taking bonus Gold during Growth is often better than growing your population, because Gold lets you buy the Progress Cards that build your engine. However, do not hoard Gold for a perfect card that might not appear. Spend Gold each round on cards that improve your position. The Progress Board refreshes every round, so cards you skip today are gone tomorrow. Aim to spend most of your Gold each round while keeping a small reserve for emergencies.

walkthrough.title: Balancing Your Resources
walkthrough.text: Nations requires you to juggle four resources and two tracks simultaneously. Gold buys cards. Stone deploys Workers and builds Wonders. Food prevents famine losses. Books generate VP at the end of each age. Military Strength protects from Wars and determines player order. Stability mitigates War losses and is needed for population growth. No single resource wins the game. You need enough of everything, with emphasis shifting over time. Early game: Gold and Food. Mid game: Stone and Military. Late game: Books and VP. The players who balance best tend to win.

walkthrough.title: Books Are King
walkthrough.text: Books generate VP four times during the game, at the end of each age. In a 4-player game, having the most Books each age could net you 3 VP four times for 12 VP total. That is enormous. Conversely, having the fewest Books each age means you gain nothing while opponents rack up points. Invest in Book production early and consistently. Even a small Book lead compounds over time. Remember that running out of Books when you owe resources causes you to lose VP and other resources instead. Protect your Book supply.

walkthrough.title: Military Is Insurance
walkthrough.text: You do not need the biggest army. You need enough Military Strength to survive Wars and Events and to get a decent player order position. Going first in player order means you get first pick of the Progress Board, which is a huge advantage. You also want enough strength to avoid being the weakest player, since many negative Events target the weakest. A moderate Military investment of one or two Military cards with Workers is usually sufficient. Only go heavy military if you plan to buy Wars yourself or need to contest Colonies with high strength requirements.

walkthrough.title: Read the Event Card Every Round
walkthrough.text: The Event Card is the most important piece of public information in the game. It tells you exactly what is coming during the Resolution Phase: how much famine will cost, which players will be rewarded or punished, and how many Architects are available. Adjust your entire round plan based on the Event Card. If famine is high, make sure you produce enough Food. If an Event rewards the most stable nation, invest in Stability. If it punishes the weakest military, deploy a Worker on a Military card. Players who ignore the Event Card get blindsided every round.

walkthrough.title: When to Pass
walkthrough.text: Passing is an action choice, and knowing when to pass is a skill. You pass when you cannot productively spend your remaining resources, when all the good Progress Cards are gone, or when taking more actions would cost you more than you gain. Sometimes passing early is strategic because you save resources for next round's Growth step and can plan for the next set of Progress Cards. Watch what your opponents are doing. If they are about to buy a War and you have enough Military, passing before they can force you into a bad position can be smart. Do not feel obligated to take actions just because you can.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Neglecting Food = famine losses every round (VP + Books)
Ignoring Military = vulnerable to Wars, stuck in last player order
Hoarding Gold = wasted resource (only used to buy cards)
Chasing Wonders before economy is running
Underestimating Books — scored 4 times, falling behind compounds

summary.title: First Round Priorities
bullets: Buy at least 1 Food or Stone Building and deploy a Worker on it
Check Event Card famine cost and plan Food coverage
Bottom-row cards (1 Gold) are great deals in round 1
Foundation first, monuments later

summary.title: Gold Management
bullets: Gold = shopping currency for Progress Cards
Early game: bonus Gold during Growth often better than growing population
Spend most Gold each round — unused Gold has minimal end-game value
Progress Board refreshes every round; skipped cards vanish

summary.title: Balancing Your Resources
bullets: 4 resources (Gold, Stone, Food, Books) + 2 tracks (Military, Stability)
Early: Gold + Food priorities; Mid: Stone + Military; Late: Books + VP
No single resource wins — balanced players win
Adjust emphasis each age based on board state

summary.title: Books Are King
bullets: Books score VP at end of each age (4 scoring rounds total)
4-player game: most Books = 3 VP per age = up to 12 VP total
Small Book lead compounds; falling behind is punishing
Running out of Books when owing resources = cascading losses

summary.title: Military Is Insurance
bullets: Need enough to survive Wars and avoid worst Events
Higher Military = better player order = first pick of cards
1-2 Military cards with Workers is usually sufficient
Go heavy only if contesting Colonies or buying Wars yourself

summary.title: Read the Event Card Every Round
bullets: Event Card shows famine cost, Events, and Architect count
Adjust entire round plan based on visible information
High famine? Ensure Food production. Event rewards stability? Invest in it
Ignoring the Event Card = getting blindsided every round

summary.title: When to Pass
bullets: Pass when you cannot productively spend remaining resources
Passing saves resources for next round's Growth
Strategic passing can avoid opponent actions (like Wars)
Do not take actions just because you can — efficiency matters

walkthrough.title: Economic Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: The economic engine approach focuses on building a self-sustaining resource machine in the first two ages. Buy Buildings that produce Gold, Food, and Stone, and deploy Workers on all of them. The goal is to generate enough surplus each Production phase that you never worry about famine, always have Gold to buy cards, and always have Stone to deploy Workers. With a strong economy, you can afford to buy top-row Progress Cards that weaker economies cannot reach. This strategy wins through efficiency: every resource you produce fuels more purchasing power next round.

walkthrough.title: Building the Economic Engine
walkthrough.text: In Age I and II, prioritize Buildings with strong production ratios: look for cards that produce 2 or more resources per Worker with low upkeep. Deploy Workers on these as soon as possible. Take bonus Gold during Growth in the early rounds rather than growing population. Only grow your population when you have Buildings waiting for Workers. By the middle of Age II, you should have three or four Buildings fully staffed and producing surplus every round. This surplus lets you pivot to Wonders and high-VP plays in Ages III and IV.

walkthrough.title: Military Dominance Strategy
walkthrough.text: The military dominance approach prioritizes Military Strength to control player order, win Events, conquer Colonies, and threaten Wars. Going first in player order is a massive advantage because you get first pick of the Progress Board before anyone else. Strong military also lets you buy Colonies with high strength requirements, which provide free production without Workers. You can also buy Wars to punish opponents who underinvest in military. The risk is that Military cards consume resources and reduce Stability. You need enough economy to support the military machine.

walkthrough.title: Executing the Military Strategy
walkthrough.text: Buy a strong Military card in Age I and deploy two Workers on it for maximum strength. Use your player order advantage to grab the best cards before opponents. Buy Colonies whenever available, as they produce for free and score VP at game end. Buy Wars when your strength is high and opponents are weak, because the VP loss hurts everyone below the War Marker. Keep your Stability high enough to mitigate War resource losses. The military strategy shines in the early and mid game but needs to transition to VP generation in Ages III and IV.

walkthrough.title: Books Accumulation Strategy
walkthrough.text: The Books strategy focuses on accumulating heritage faster than everyone else. Since Books are scored four times throughout the game, a consistent Book lead is worth enormous VP. Target Buildings, Wonders, and Advisors that produce Books. Some Advisors double down on Books with special abilities. The key insight is that Books both score VP and serve as a buffer against resource shortfalls. When you cannot pay a cost, you lose Books instead. So a large Book reserve protects you from cascading losses during bad rounds.

walkthrough.title: Wonder Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: Wonders provide VP, special abilities, and sometimes ongoing production. The wonder rush focuses on completing Wonders quickly by ensuring Stone supply and Architect access. Some Wonders give you private Architects, making future Wonders easier to build. The trick is timing. You need an economy that generates enough Stone to pay construction costs while also covering your other needs. Start a Wonder in Age I only if you can complete it in 2-3 rounds. In later ages, powerful Wonders like Versailles or the Taj Mahal can swing the game if you finish them quickly.

walkthrough.title: Adaptive Play: Reading the Table
walkthrough.text: The strongest Nations players do not commit to one strategy. They read the table and adapt. If an opponent is investing heavily in Military, do not compete for strength directly. Instead, maintain enough to survive and outpace them on Books and economy. If nobody is investing in Military, a small investment lets you dominate player order cheaply. Watch what cards opponents take from the Progress Board. If they are buying all the Food Buildings, invest in Stone and Gold Buildings instead. The Progress Board is a shared market, and understanding supply and demand gives you an edge.

walkthrough.title: Player Order Manipulation
walkthrough.text: Player order in Nations is determined by Military Strength. Going first means you see the full Progress Board before anyone else takes cards. This is worth a lot. Even if you do not want a military-focused strategy, maintaining enough strength to go first or second can win you the game. Consider deploying just one Worker on a Military card in the early rounds and keeping it there as a baseline. When Event Cards show events that benefit the first or second player, being in position to claim them adds up over eight rounds.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Pivots
walkthrough.text: Ages III and IV are where games are won or lost. The Progress Cards become more powerful but also more expensive. Your economy from the early game should now be self-sustaining. Shift your focus to VP generation: Wonders with VP, Buildings and Military with VP for deployed Workers, Golden Ages that convert resources to VP, and maintaining your Book lead. In the final round, sometimes it is worth undeploying Workers from Military or Buildings that no longer matter and redeploying them on cards with end-game VP bonuses. Every point counts.

walkthrough.title: Resource Conversion Efficiency
walkthrough.text: At the expert level, Nations is about conversion rates. Gold converts to Progress Cards. Stone converts to Worker deployment and Wonder sections. Food prevents famine losses. Books convert to VP every age. Military converts to player order and War survival. The best players calculate the VP value of every action. Buying a Golden Age that converts 3 resources to 1 VP is good if those resources are surplus. Buying a Colony that produces 2 Food per round for the rest of the game is worth more than its Gold cost over time. Always think in terms of VP per action and VP per resource.

summary.title: Economic Engine Strategy
bullets: Build self-sustaining resource production in Ages I-II
Surplus Gold, Food, Stone = affordability of top-row cards
Strong economy powers everything else in the game
Win through efficiency and purchasing power

summary.title: Building the Economic Engine
bullets: Prioritize Buildings with 2+ resources per Worker, low upkeep
Take bonus Gold in Growth over population early on
Only grow population when you have Buildings waiting for Workers
By mid-Age II: 3-4 staffed Buildings producing surplus

summary.title: Military Dominance Strategy
bullets: Highest Military = first player order = first pick of Progress Board
Strong military enables Colonies, Wars, and Event rewards
Risk: Military consumes resources and can lower Stability
Need enough economy to sustain the military machine

summary.title: Executing the Military Strategy
bullets: Buy strong Military card in Age I, deploy 2 Workers for max strength
Conquer Colonies for free production; buy Wars to punish opponents
Maintain Stability to mitigate your own War resource losses
Transition from military control to VP generation in Ages III-IV

summary.title: Books Accumulation Strategy
bullets: Books scored 4 times = consistent lead is worth massive VP
Target Book-producing Buildings, Wonders, and Advisors
Large Book reserve buffers against cascading resource losses
Book lead compounds across all four ages

summary.title: Wonder Rush Strategy
bullets: Wonders = VP + special abilities + sometimes production
Need steady Stone supply and Architect access
Some Wonders grant private Architects, enabling more Wonders
Timing critical: start early only if you can finish in 2-3 rounds

summary.title: Adaptive Play: Reading the Table
bullets: Don't lock into one strategy — read opponents and adapt
If opponent goes heavy Military, outpace on Books/economy instead
Watch what cards opponents take — exploit gaps in supply
Progress Board is a shared market; understanding demand gives edge

summary.title: Player Order Manipulation
bullets: First player order = first pick of Progress Board each round
Even non-military strategies benefit from going first or second
1 Worker on Military as baseline investment in player order
Events that reward first/second player add up over 8 rounds

summary.title: Late-Game Pivots
bullets: Ages III-IV: shift from engine-building to VP generation
Wonders with VP, Golden Ages, Building/Military VP for Workers
Final round: redeploy Workers to cards with end-game VP bonuses
Every point counts — optimize final positions

summary.title: Resource Conversion Efficiency
bullets: Expert play = calculating VP value of every action
Gold -> cards, Stone -> deployment, Food -> survival, Books -> VP
Compare VP per action and VP per resource for every option
Surplus resources into Golden Age VP conversions in late game

entries.definition: One of four resources. Used to buy Progress Cards from the Progress Board. Top row costs 3 Gold, middle 2, bottom 1. Gold has limited end-game value (counts toward Resource VP at 1 VP per 10 total resources).

One of four resources. Required to pay famine costs at the end of every round and to grow population from the Food section of the Population Track. Failure to pay Food costs results in VP and Book losses.
One of four resources. Used to deploy Workers on Buildings and Military and to construct Wonder sections. Stone is the currency of action: without it, you cannot activate your cards.

One of four resources. Represents your nation's heritage. Accumulated Books are scored at the end of each age: you gain 1 VP for every opponent with fewer Books than you. Also serves as a buffer against resource shortfalls.

The currency of winning. VP tokens are gained and lost during the game from Events, Wars, Books scoring, Golden Ages, and other effects. Final score includes Game VP plus Colonies, Wonders, Buildings/Military, and Resource VP.
A tracked value on the Score Board, not a resource. Increased by deploying Workers on Military cards. Determines player order, War outcomes, Colony eligibility, and Event effects. Caps at 40.

A tracked value on the Score Board. Increased by some Buildings and Progress Cards. Decreased by taking Workers from the Stability section of the Population Track. Mitigates War resource losses. Negative Stability puts you in Revolt. Caps at 15, minimum tracked is -3 but actual value can be lower.
The track on the Score Board where players mark their accumulated Books using colored discs. Players with higher disc positions have more Books. Used for end-of-age scoring comparisons.
A track on the Score Board showing the current turn order. Updated during the Resolution Phase based on Military Strength. Higher Military Strength means earlier player order.

A state triggered by negative Stability. All nations in Revolt are considered tied for lowest Stability for Event purposes. Nations in Revolt lose 1 Book per negative Stability point and 1 VP during Production.

A type of Progress Card placed on Building/Military slots on your Player Board. Workers deployed on Buildings produce resources during the Production step and may increase Stability. Buildings without Workers have no effect.

A type of Progress Card sharing slots with Buildings. Workers on Military increase Military Strength and give the card a Raid value for Battles. Military often has upkeep costs in resources or Stability. Military without Workers has no effect.

A Progress Card with a Military Strength requirement. Once bought, produces resources during Production without needing Workers. You can have up to 2 Colonies; a 3rd requires removing one. Scores VP at game end.

A Progress Card any player can buy (max 1 per round). Placed on the War slot with the War Marker set to the buyer's Military Strength. In Resolution, nations below the marker are defeated, losing 1 VP and resources. Stability reduces resource loss but not VP loss.

A Progress Card that gives an immediate bonus of Books, Food, or Stone equal to your best Military's Raid value, then is removed. Requires at least one Worker deployed on a Military card. You choose one resource type only.

A Progress Card built in sections using Architects and Stone. Placed on the Wonder under construction space. Only 1 can be under construction at a time. When complete, moves to a Wonder slot and activates special effects. Max 5 completed Wonders.

A Progress Card placed on the Advisor slot. You can have only 1 Advisor at a time (unless a card says otherwise). Advisors provide ongoing abilities, production, or end-game VP. A new Advisor permanently replaces the old one.

A Progress Card offering a choice: take the printed resources, or pay a cost in any resource combination for 1 VP. Card is removed after use. Golden Age bonus from other cards increases the resources gained or reduces the VP cost.

Drawn each round during Maintenance. Shows two Events (resolved in Resolution), the famine Food cost, and extra Architects for the round. Visible to all players, allowing preparation during the Action Phase.

The marketplace where Progress Cards are displayed for purchase. Three rows: top costs 3 Gold, middle 2 Gold, bottom 1 Gold. Refreshed each round. Number of columns depends on player count (2p=4, 3p=5, 4p=6, 5p=7).
Central board with the Round Track, Heritage Track, Military Strength Track, Stability Track, Player Order Track, Architect spaces, War slot, and Event Card slot.

Each player's personal nation board. Has slots for Advisor (1), Colonies (2), Buildings/Military (5), Wonder under construction (1), completed Wonders (5), Population Track, and Resource Area. A-side for beginners, B-side for experienced players.

Located on your Player Board, divided into Food section and Stability section (4 spaces each). Workers taken from the Food section cost 3 Food upkeep during Production. Workers taken from the Stability section reduce Stability by 3 immediately.
The area on your Player Board where you keep your resource tokens and available Workers. Workers in the Resource Area can be deployed onto Buildings and Military.

Represent your population. 13 per player. Deployed on Buildings and Military to activate them. Taken from the Population Track during Growth (increasing Food upkeep or reducing Stability). Cannot be deployed on Colonies, Wonders, or Advisors.

Tokens used to build Wonder sections. Taken from the Architect spaces on the Score Board. When a Wonder is complete, Architects are returned to the Architect Supply. 15 total in the game. Some Progress Cards grant private Architects.

Placing a Worker on a Building or Military Progress Card and paying its Stone cost. Workers can come from the Resource Area or another card. Only 1 Worker can be deployed per turn. Multiplies the card's effect.

Moving a Worker from a Building or Military back to the Resource Area. Not an action; can be done at any time during your turn. You can undeploy multiple Workers at once. The card's benefit is lost immediately.

Taking an Architect from the Score Board, placing it on the leftmost empty section of your Wonder under construction, and paying the section's Stone cost. Constructs one section of a Wonder per action.
Ending your participation in the current round's Action Phase. Once you pass, you cannot take any more actions this round. Sometimes strategic to save resources or avoid opponent actions.

A number on Military cards used when buying Battle Progress Cards. Your Raid value equals the printed value on your best Military card with at least one Worker. Multiple Workers do not increase Raid value. Determines how many Books, Food, or Stone you gain from a Battle.

At the end of every round, all nations must pay a Food cost shown on the Event Card. Failure to pay results in losing remaining Food, 1 VP, and 1 Book per missing Food. Famine costs generally increase through the ages.

The first step of the Resolution Phase. All nations simultaneously calculate their resource gains and consumption from Buildings, Military, Colonies, Wonders, and Advisors. Resources are processed in order: Gold, Food, Stone, Books.

Critical rule: for positive effects, you must win alone. Tied players receive nothing. For negative effects, all players tied for last place suffer the full penalty. All nations in Revolt are tied for lowest Stability regardless of actual value.

When you owe resources you do not have, you lose what you have plus 1 VP and 1 Book per missing resource. If your Books are at 0 and should decrease further, you lose 1 VP and 1 other resource of your choice per Book owed. Maximum 1 VP loss per resource type per round.

The first age, covering rounds 1-2. Represents the earliest historical period (before 4000 BCE to 500 CE). Progress Cards are the weakest but cheapest. Sets the foundation for your nation's development.
The second age, covering rounds 3-4. Represents 500-1450 CE. Stronger Progress Cards become available. Nations should have functioning economies by now. Competition for Events and Wars intensifies.
The third age, covering rounds 5-6. Represents 1450-1750 CE. Powerful Wonders and Advisors appear. The pivot from engine-building to VP generation typically happens here.
The fourth and final age, covering rounds 7-8. Represents 1750-1913 CE. The most powerful cards with the highest costs and effects. Final round decisions determine the winner.

Four levels: Chieftain (easiest, most bonus resources), Prince, King, Emperor (hardest, fewest bonus resources). Affects only the Growth step bonus. Acts as a handicap system allowing mixed-experience groups to play together.
The easiest difficulty level. Gives the most bonus resources during the Growth step (typically 5). Recommended for first-time players.
The second difficulty level. Gives moderate bonus resources during Growth (typically 3). A good step up after a few games.
The third difficulty level. Gives fewer bonus resources during Growth. For experienced players comfortable with the game's economy.
The hardest difficulty level. Gives the fewest bonus resources during Growth (typically 1). For expert players seeking maximum challenge.

A black marker placed on the Military Strength Track when a War is bought. Set to the buyer's current Military Strength. Nations below this value in Resolution are defeated. Does not move if the buyer's strength changes after purchase.
Progress Cards with no special symbol. Used in all games. Recommended for learning games with only base cards included.
Progress Cards marked with an advanced symbol. Add more interaction and variation. Mix into base cards after a few learning games.
Progress Cards marked with an expert symbol. Add the most complex interactions and new concepts. Mix in after playing with advanced cards. Required for 5-player games.

Each Player Board has two sides. A-side is standard for learning. B-side adds special nation-specific rules, different starting resources, and unique abilities. B-sides are chosen in reverse player order after seeing the Progress Board.
Placed on Progress Cards with special actions to indicate the action has been used this round. Removed at the end of the round. Prevents using the same special action multiple times per round.
First category of final scoring. The total VP tokens accumulated during the game from Events, Wars, Books scoring, Golden Ages, and other in-game effects.
Second category of final scoring. VP printed on your Colony cards at game end.
Third category of final scoring. VP from completed Wonders on your Player Board, including any special VP bonuses they grant.
Fourth category of final scoring. VP from Workers deployed on Building and Military cards. The first Worker gives the leftmost VP value, second Worker the next, etc. Cards without Workers score 0.
Fifth category of final scoring. Add Gold + Food + Stone + Books + Military Strength + Stability. For each full 10 of this sum, gain 1 VP.
